context.description: A philosophical prose passage criticizes "dream philosophers" who seek comfort, unity, and pain relief instead of confronting inner contradictions. It argues that positive altered-state experiences can function like deeper sleep, while horror trips and discomfort reveal the risks of self-deception.
normalization_notes: Source language is German; summary translated into English.
No explicit chapter heading or title visible on the supplied page.

rows.paragraph_text: The self is stabilized by a drive to appear contradiction-free, yet humans are not remotely free of contradiction.
A comforting feeling of oneness after such an experience is framed as a deeper retreat into dreamlike self-concealment, where contradictions are less visible and pain is easier to avoid.

The author calls such enthusiasts "dream philosophers," warning that only pleasant experiences are valued while horror trips are treated as failures, even though these experiences may carry real psychological risk.
The passage closes with a self-aware challenge to this one-sided preference for comfort and a call to face the harsh reality outside the blanket of self-soothing.
